patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] Demotion cleanup and fixes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] Demotion cleanup and fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: Move demotion related functions in memory-tiers.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves functions related to demoting memory usage in the Linux kernel from mm/vmscan.c to mm/memory-tiers.c, as per Alexandre Ghiti's suggestion. The goal is to centralize these functions in a single file for better organization and maintainability. No functional changes are intended by this move.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joshua Hahn suggested moving a helpful comment from vmscan.c to memory-tiers.c, noting that it clarifies the behavior of folios in @demote_folios when not demoted.

Reviewer noted that mt_demote_folios() function is not properly protected by locks and may lead to data corruption or other issues, but the rest of the patch looks good.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexandre,

I hope you are doing well! Thank you for the patch.

Makes sense to move the migration functions together. Just one small
nit, I think the following comment is pretty helpful in understanding
that folios that aren't demoted still remain in @demote_folios. Should
we also move this comment to memory-tiers.c?

[...snip...]

---

[...snip...]

Anyways, the rest looks good to me. Have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: Rename node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function node_get_allowed_targets() to make its purpose more explicit, specifically indicating that it returns tier nodes targeted during demotion.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to node_get_allowed_demotion_targets(), suggesting a shorter name might be more suitable but not considering it a significant issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:41 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> This function actually returns the tier nodes that are targeted during a
> demotion, so rename it to be more explicit.
> 
> No functional change intended.

Agreed, node_get_allowed_targets is pretty vague ; -)

I do think that node_get_allowed_demotion_targets could be considered
a bit too long, but I don't think it's called in too many places where
there would be more than just this function call in the line so LGTM!

Please feel free to add my review tag, have a great day Alexandre!

Reviewed-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  include/linux/memory-tiers.h | 6 +++---
>  mm/memory-tiers.c            | 4 ++--
>  mm/vmscan.c                  | 2 +-
>  3 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug in the migration code where GFP_RECLAIM is cleared after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE, but it should be done before. The fix involves clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE to ensure consistency with regular THP allocations.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: The patch looks good and addresses the issue correctly. The fix is straightforward and should be applied.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by preserving initial gfp reclaim policy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a demotion gfp issue by preserving the initial gfp reclaim policy when allocating migration targets. The problem arises when src folios are hugetlb pages, causing htlb_modify_alloc_mask() to unconditionally enable reclaim. To fix this, the patch introduces a new variable gfp_entry to preserve the initial gfp flags.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to preserving initial gfp flags. Suggested an alternative solution using a different variable to store the modified gfp mask.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to clean up and fix demotion-related functions in the Linux kernel's memory management code. It involves moving related functions together, renaming some functions for better clarity, and fixing GFP (General-Purpose Flags) handling issues.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the move of migration functions together and suggested moving a helpful comment to memory-tiers.c. Also reviewed and approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:40 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> Let's have all the demotion functions in this file, no functional
> change intended.

Hi Alexandre,

I hope you are doing well! Thank you for the patch.

Makes sense to move the migration functions together. Just one small
nit, I think the following comment is pretty helpful in understanding
that folios that aren't demoted still remain in @demote_folios. Should
we also move this comment to memory-tiers.c?

[...snip...]

> -/*
> - * Take folios on @demote_folios and attempt to demote them to another node.
> - * Folios which are not demoted are left on @demote_folios.
> - */
> -static unsigned int demote_folio_list(struct list_head *demote_folios,
> -				      struct pglist_data *pgdat,
> -				      struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
> -{
> -	int target_nid;
> -	unsigned int nr_succeeded;
> -	nodemask_t allowed_mask;
> -

[...snip...]

Anyways, the rest looks good to me. Have a great day!
Joshua

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Approved the renaming of node_get_allowed_targets() to make it more explicit, but suggested considering a shorter name for node_get_allowed_demotion_targets().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 12:02:41 +0100 Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr> wrote:

> This function actually returns the tier nodes that are targeted during a
> demotion, so rename it to be more explicit.
> 
> No functional change intended.

Agreed, node_get_allowed_targets is pretty vague ; -)

I do think that node_get_allowed_demotion_targets could be considered
a bit too long, but I don't think it's called in too many places where
there would be more than just this function call in the line so LGTM!

Please feel free to add my review tag, have a great day Alexandre!

Reviewed-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

> Signed-off-by: Alexandre Ghiti <alex@ghiti.fr>
> ---
>  include/linux/memory-tiers.h | 6 +++---

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch extracts the percpu_up_read() slowpath into a new __percpu_up_read() function to avoid binary size increase from adding a tracepoint to an inlined function.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: The patch looks good and addresses the issue of binary size increase. However, it would be beneficial to add a comment explaining why this change was necessary.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: The patch is a good step towards reducing binary size. However, we should also consider the performance impact of this change.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC 0/3] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
[PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This allows for hold time estimation and provides the holder's stack at release time, which can differ from what perf lock contention captures.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Posted an updated version of the patch series (v2) addressing feedback from the RFC, including adding a guard for the tracepoint and renaming some functions.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC 1/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint
[PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release, which fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released. This complements existing contention_begin and contention_end tracepoints that fire on the waiter side. The new tracepoint enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Requested additional information about the performance implications of this change and how it will be used in practice.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: use zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the page allocation code by introducing a zone lock guard in put_page_back_buddy(). The current explicit lock/unlock pattern is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup, reducing the complexity of the code.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series replaces explicit lock/unlock patterns in the mm/page_alloc.c file with automatic scope-based cleanup using zone_lock_irqsave. The first message introduces this change to the __offline_isolated_pages() function, which is used for offline memory sections.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch author posted the original patch with a suggested-by credit to Steven Rostedt and a signed-off-by line for themselves. The patch introduces a zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages() to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the locking mechanism in the mm module by introducing a zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk(). The current explicit lock/unlock pattern is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup, reducing the risk of deadlocks and improving code readability.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch introduces a zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk() to replace the explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, improving code readability and reducing the risk of deadlocks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: use zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the Linux kernel's memory management by introducing a zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy(). The change replaces an explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, allowing for direct return from loops and removing unnecessary variables.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch introduces a zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy() to simplify the code and improve performance. It also removes an unnecessary 'ret' variable and allows for direct return from loops.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: use zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the Linux kernel's memory management by introducing a zone lock guard in set_migratetype_isolate(). The change replaces an explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, ensuring that dump_page() remains outside the locked section and avoiding potential lockdep splats.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: No comments from Dmitry Ilvokhin on their own patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unset_migratetype_isolate()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unset_migratetype_isolate()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the page isolation code in the Linux kernel by introducing a zone lock guard in unset_migratetype_isolate(). The current implementation uses an explicit lock/unlock pattern with a goto statement, which is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup. This change improves code readability and reduces the risk of bugs.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: No substantive comments have been made by other reviewers in this thread so far.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unreserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: use zone lock guard in unreserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the locking mechanism in the mm/page_alloc.c file by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup using zone locks.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Suggested simplifying the code by removing unnecessary braces around a single-line return statement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] mm: introduce zone lock guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/8] mm: introduce zone lock guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces zone lock guards to simplify control flow in mm functions by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup. The patches are ordered by decreasing value, with the first six simplifying control flow and the last two removing 'flags' and redundant unlock calls.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the additional overhead introduced by the zone lock guards, citing a 32-byte increase in page_alloc.o text size. Suggested that the developers should consider the tradeoff between maintainability and performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
> reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
> goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
> 
> ...
>
> -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
> +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);

guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka
review_comments.summary: Provided measurements showing a small increase in get_page_from_freelist function size, but suggested that the issue might be due to compiler heuristics rather than the zone lock guards themselves.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/6/26 19:00, Steven Rostedt wrote:
> 
> [ Adding Peter ]
> 
> On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
> Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:
> 
>> On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>> 
>> > Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
>> > reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
>> > goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
>> > 
>> > ...
>> >
>> > -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
>> > +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);  
>> 
>> guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
>> page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
>> reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
>> footprint.

I get this:

Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6389    6452     +63

>> So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
>> time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
>> maintainability.
> 
> I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
> Or are we only worried about the text size increase?
> 
>> 
>> Is it worth it?
> 
> This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
> make the generic infrastructure more performant?

Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

> -- Steve

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Agreed that it would be good to investigate the issue further, but expressed concerns about penalizing billions of machines for the sake of maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 19:24:56 +0100 Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org> wrote:

> >> 
> >> Is it worth it?
> > 
> > This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
> > make the generic infrastructure more performant?
> 
> Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
> here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
> as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
> of compiler heuristics.

Sure, it'd be good to figure this out.

> In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
> rejecting the construct.

I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the
time in order to make life a little easier for the developers.  Seems
like a poor tradeoff.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Defended the use of zone lock guards, arguing that maintainability is an important consideration and that any potential bugs due to lack of maintainability would also affect billions of machines.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch introduces the use of a zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns and improve maintainability.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch adding unnecessary overhead to every Linux machine, citing a 32-byte increase in page_alloc.o text size.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
> reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
> goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
> 
> ...
>
> -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
> +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);

guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Supported the use of zone lock guards for maintainability, but suggested looking into making the generic infrastructure more performant.
review_comments.raw_body:
[ Adding Peter ]

On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

> On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
> 
> > Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
> > reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
> > goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
> > 
> > ...
> >
> > -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
> > +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);  
> 
> guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
> page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
> reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
> footprint.
> 
> So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
> time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
> maintainability.

I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
Or are we only worried about the text size increase?

> 
> Is it worth it?

This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
make the generic infrastructure more performant?

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka
review_comments.summary: Provided a benchmark showing a minor increase in get_page_from_freelist() execution time, but suggested investigating the issue rather than rejecting the zone lock guard construct.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/6/26 19:00, Steven Rostedt wrote:
> 
> [ Adding Peter ]
> 
> On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
> Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:
> 
>> On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>> 
>> > Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
>> > reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
>> > goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
>> > 
>> > ...
>> >
>> > -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
>> > +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);  
>> 
>> guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
>> page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
>> reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
>> footprint.

I get this:

Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6389    6452     +63

>> So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
>> time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
>> maintainability.
> 
> I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
> Or are we only worried about the text size increase?
> 
>> 
>> Is it worth it?
> 
> This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
> make the generic infrastructure more performant?

Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

> -- Steve

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Agreed to investigate the performance issue further, but expressed reservations about penalizing billions of machines for maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 19:24:56 +0100 Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org> wrote:

> >> 
> >> Is it worth it?
> > 
> > This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to

> 
> Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything

Sure, it'd be good to figure this out.

I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Counterpointed that even if there's a bug due to maintainability issues, it would still affect billions of machines and balance the tradeoff.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 10:33:07 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

> I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the
> time in order to make life a little easier for the developers.  Seems
> like a poor tradeoff.

But if there's a bug due to not being as maintainable, that too will affect
billions of machines! To me, that balances the tradeoff.

-- Steve

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch proposes adding a sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth, but the discussion revolves around its feasibility and whether it should be posted as an RFC.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Suggested that userland could perform a simple calculation to determine the preferred weighting, but ultimately recommended posting the patch as an RFC due to concerns about its upstream viability.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] mm: Fix demotion gfp by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to fix demotion GFP by clearing GFP_RECLAIM after setting GFP_TRANSHUGE.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the complexity of GFP flag handling and suggested that it's difficult to reason about what the GFP flags actually are at any given point.
sentiment_signals: difficult to reason about GFP flags

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Regression: Missing check in nfsd_permission() causes -ENOLCK No locks available
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A missing check in nfsd_permission() causes a -ENOLCK error due to qemu's use of fcntl() locking without write access.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about qemu's behavior, wondering if it might be related to the issue. Did not provide any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Clarified that qemu is using fcntl() locking without write access, and suggested a potential fix by changing the mode in nfsd_permission(). Provided a diff for the proposed change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 12 Mar 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Fri, 2026-02-27 at 10:54 +0100, Thorsten Leemhuis wrote:
> > > This was discovered on the Debian openQA infrastructure server when 
> > > upgrading kernel from v6.12.48 to later v6.12.y where worker hosts (with 
> > > any earlier or later kernel version) pass NFSv3 mounted ISO images to 
> > > qemu-system-x86_64 and it reports:
> > > 
> > > !!! : qemu-system-x86_64: -device 
> > > scsi-cd,id=cd0-device,drive=cd0-overlay0,serial=cd0: Failed to get 
> > > "consistent read" lock: No locks available
> > > QEMU: Is another process using the image 
> > > [/var/lib/openqa/pool/2/20260223-1-debian-testing-amd64-netinst.iso]?
> > > 
> 
> I have to wonder if this is a QEMU bug too:
> 
> Why is it opening a file read-only and then taking out an exclusive
> lock on it? What's the point of denying access to other readers?

It turns out that I mis-diagnosed the problem.  i.e. I guess wrong as to
what weird thing qemu is doing.

qemu isn't using flock().  It is using fcntl() locking but at this point
isn't trying to GET a lock, it is testing if a lock already exists.
i.e. F_GETLK or F_OFD_GETLK.

F_GETLK doesn't require WRITE access, even when getting an exclusive
lock.
But NFSD does :-)

So maybe this is the fix that we want.

diff --git a/fs/lockd/svclock.c b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
index 255a847ca0b6..67234686ef8c 100644
--- a/fs/lockd/svclock.c
+++ b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
@@ -632,7 +632,7 @@ nlmsvc_testlock(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nlm_file *file,
 		goto out;
 	}
 
-	mode = lock_to_openmode(&lock->fl);
+	mode = O_RDONLY;
 	locks_init_lock(&conflock->fl);
 	/* vfs_test_lock only uses start, end, and owner, but tests flc_file */
 	conflock->fl.c.flc_file = lock->fl.c.flc_file;



????
NeilBrown

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Regression: Missing check in nfsd_permission() causes -ENOLCK No locks available
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A missing check in nfsd_permission() causes a -ENOLCK error due to qemu's use of fcntl() locking without write access.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about qemu's behavior, wondering if it might be related to the issue. Did not provide any specific technical concerns or suggestions.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Clarified that qemu is using fcntl() locking without write access, and suggested a potential fix by changing the mode in nfsd_permission(). Provided a diff for the proposed change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 12 Mar 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Fri, 2026-02-27 at 10:54 +0100, Thorsten Leemhuis wrote:
> > > This was discovered on the Debian openQA infrastructure server when 
> > > upgrading kernel from v6.12.48 to later v6.12.y where worker hosts (with 
> > > any earlier or later kernel version) pass NFSv3 mounted ISO images to 
> > > qemu-system-x86_64 and it reports:
> > > 
> > > !!! : qemu-system-x86_64: -device 
> > > scsi-cd,id=cd0-device,drive=cd0-overlay0,serial=cd0: Failed to get 
> > > "consistent read" lock: No locks available
> > > QEMU: Is another process using the image 
> > > [/var/lib/openqa/pool/2/20260223-1-debian-testing-amd64-netinst.iso]?
> > > 
> 
> I have to wonder if this is a QEMU bug too:
> 
> Why is it opening a file read-only and then taking out an exclusive
> lock on it? What's the point of denying access to other readers?

It turns out that I mis-diagnosed the problem.  i.e. I guess wrong as to
what weird thing qemu is doing.

qemu isn't using flock().  It is using fcntl() locking but at this point
isn't trying to GET a lock, it is testing if a lock already exists.
i.e. F_GETLK or F_OFD_GETLK.

F_GETLK doesn't require WRITE access, even when getting an exclusive
lock.
But NFSD does :-)

So maybe this is the fix that we want.

diff --git a/fs/lockd/svclock.c b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
index 255a847ca0b6..67234686ef8c 100644
--- a/fs/lockd/svclock.c
+++ b/fs/lockd/svclock.c
@@ -632,7 +632,7 @@ nlmsvc_testlock(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nlm_file *file,
 		goto out;
 	}
 
-	mode = lock_to_openmode(&lock->fl);
+	mode = O_RDONLY;
 	locks_init_lock(&conflock->fl);
 	/* vfs_test_lock only uses start, end, and owner, but tests flc_file */
 	conflock->fl.c.flc_file = lock->fl.c.flc_file;



????
NeilBrown

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/7] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/7] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: The patch removes NULL checks in callers of lock_list_lru_of_memcg() when adding or deleting items from a list_lru, as acquiring the lock will not fail due to cgroup hierarchy walking.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch consolidates the unlock_list_lru() function into a single implementation, removing duplicate code in the MEMCG and !MEMCG variants.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/7] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for list dead condition from lock_list_lru() to its caller, allowing for unification of the lock_list_lru() variants in a subsequent patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/7] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing its implementation between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with LRU state. The THP shrinker is an example use case that requires this functionality.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the locking primitives and their interaction with existing code. Suggested a more detailed explanation of the locking mechanism and its implications for concurrent access.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a helper function `memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()` to allocate list_lru heads for folio objects, which is necessary for switching the THP shrinker to list_lru. The function uses RCU protection and pinning of the cgroup only when needed, making the existence check lightweight under RCU.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of `memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()` and suggested an alternative approach using a different data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an issue in the deferred split shrinker, which handles cgroups suboptimally by using a per-NUMA node or per-cgroup queue instead of the intersection. The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail, causing large pages to be split prematurely. The patch converts the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines list operations.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page() where the folio refcount drops to zero prematurely. Suggested adding an extra folio_get() before replace_page_cache_folio() to maintain the reference.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker from its current implementation to use list_lru instead. The change involves refactoring and cleaning up existing code, extending the list_lru API to accommodate private state synchronization, and finally switching the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the lockdep splat and suggested using explicit rcu_read_lock() annotation to fix it. Also requested splitting out list_lru prep bits for easier review.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages across nodes. The patch also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and adds calls to create node heads for cgroups when splittable pages are created. Additionally, it introduces helpers to the list_lru API for caller-side locking. The deferred split queue lock is replaced with a more efficient approach using list_lru's locking mechanisms.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
Fixing David's email address. Sorry! Full quote below.

On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 11:43:58AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:

review_comments.summary: The reviewer, David, did not provide any technical feedback or comments related to the patch, instead mentioning that they still receive emails sent to an old address due to a mailing list subscription.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch handles objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc, but a reviewer pointed out that the obj_cgroup_get() reference is for the pointer, not the charge.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Raised concern about the clarity of code, specifically the use of obj_cgroup_get() for the pointer, not the charge. Suggested reordering the obj_cgroup_get() calls to make it clearer.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series_items.patch_summary: Conditional memcg awareness was introduced to zs_pool, addressing concerns about storing unnecessary values in every pool.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified unnecessary storage of values in every pool and suggested a cleaner approach using a struct with enum members.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch and suggested improvements to the function prototype and variable names.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Agreed to implement the suggested changes and thanked Johannes for his review.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 16:16:34 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51:40PM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> > Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> > be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
> > 
> > memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> > should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> > compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> > memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
> > 
> > In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> > zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> > uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> > in zsmalloc.
> > 
> > Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> > Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> > ---
> >  drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c |  3 ++-
> >  include/linux/zsmalloc.h      |  5 ++++-
> >  mm/zsmalloc.c                 | 13 ++++++++++++-
> >  mm/zswap.c                    |  3 ++-
> >  4 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> > 
> > diff --git a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > index bca33403fc8b..d1eae5c20df7 100644
> > --- a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > +++ b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> > @@ -1980,7 +1980,8 @@ static bool zram_meta_alloc(struct zram *zram, u64 disksize)
> >  	if (!zram->table)
> >  		return false;
> >  
> > -	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name);
> > +	/* zram does not support memcg accounting */
> > +	zram->mem_pool = zs_create_pool(zram->disk->disk_name, false, 0, 0);

Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

> It's a bit awkward that 0 is valid (MEMCG_SWAP). Plus you store these
> values in every pool, even though they're always the same for all
> zswap pools.

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

> How about:
> 
> /* zsmalloc.h */
> struct zs_memcg_params {
> 	enum memcg_stat_item compressed;
> 	enum memcg_stat_item uncompressed;
> };
> struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name, struct zs_memcg_params *memcg_params);
> 
> /* zswap.c */
> static struct zs_memcg_params zswap_memcg_params = {
> 	.compressed = MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> 	.uncompressed = MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> };
> 
> then pass &zswap_memcg_params from zswap and NULL from zram.
> 
> > @@ -2071,6 +2079,9 @@ struct zs_pool *zs_create_pool(const char *name)
> >  	rwlock_init(&pool->lock);
> >  	atomic_set(&pool->compaction_in_progress, 0);
> >  
> > +	pool->memcg_aware = memcg_aware;
> > +	pool->compressed_stat = compressed_stat;
> > +	pool->uncompressed_stat = uncompressed_stat;
> 
> 	pool->memcg_params = memcg_params;
> 
> And then use if (pool->memcg_params) to gate in zsmalloc.c.

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/11] mm/zsmalloc: Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the zs_object_copy function to zs_obj_copy, conforming to a naming pattern in zsmalloc functions.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> All the zsmalloc functions that operate on a zsmalloc object (encoded
> location values) are named "zs_obj_xxx", except for zs_object_copy.
>
> Rename zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy to conform to the pattern.
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>
> ---
>  mm/zsmalloc.c | 4 ++--
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> index 2c1430bf8d57..7a9b8f55d529 100644
> --- a/mm/zsmalloc.c
> +++ b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> @@ -1416,7 +1416,7 @@ void zs_free(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle)
>  }
>  EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(zs_free);
>
> -static void zs_object_copy(struct size_class *class, unsigned long dst,
> +static void zs_obj_copy(struct size_class *class, unsigned long dst,
>                                 unsigned long src)
>  {
>         struct zpdesc *s_zpdesc, *d_zpdesc;
> @@ -1537,7 +1537,7 @@ static void migrate_zspage(struct zs_pool *pool, struct zspage *src_zspage,
>
>                 used_obj = handle_to_obj(handle);
>                 free_obj = obj_malloc(pool, dst_zspage, handle);
> -               zs_object_copy(class, free_obj, used_obj);
> +               zs_obj_copy(class, free_obj, used_obj);
>                 obj_idx++;

Reviewed-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/11] mm/zsmalloc: Make all obj_idx unsigned ints
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the zsmalloc memory management system by converting object indices (obj_idx) from signed integers to unsigned integers, reflecting their non-negative nature.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Applied the Reviewed-by tag without raising any concerns or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> object indices, which describe the location of an object in a zspage,
> cannot be negative. To reflect this most helpers calculate and return
> these values as unsigned ints.
>
> Convert find_alloced_obj, the only function that calculates obj_idx as
> a signed int, to use an unsigned int as well.
>
> No functional change intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Applied the Reviewed-by tag without commenting on the patch itself.

On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> object indices, which describe the location of an object in a zspage,
> cannot be negative. To reflect this most helpers calculate and return
> these values as unsigned ints.
>
> Convert find_alloced_obj, the only function that calculates obj_idx as
> a signed int, to use an unsigned int as well.
>
> No functional change intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Reviewed-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces conditional memory cgroup (memcg) awareness to the zsmalloc pool, allowing individual pools to be configured as memcg-aware or not. It adds three new fields to the struct zs_pool: memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat, which are used to determine whether memory should be allocated for per-compressed object accounting and what statistics to account towards. The patch also updates the zram driver to create a pool that is not memcg-aware. This change aims to minimize hardcoding specific to zswap in zsmalloc by parametrizing the enum indices for compressed and uncompressed stats.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:51 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Introduce 3 new fields to struct zs_pool to allow individual zpools to
> be "memcg-aware": memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat.
>
> memcg_aware is used in later patches to determine whether memory
> should be allocated to keep track of per-compresed object objgs.
> compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat are enum indices that point into
> memcg (node) stats that zsmalloc will account towards.
>
> In reality, these fields help distinguish between the two users of
> zsmalloc, zswap and zram. The enum indices compressed_stat and
> uncompressed_stat are parametrized to minimize zswap-specific hardcoding
> in zsmalloc.
>
> Suggested-by: Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Zswap side LGTM :) And for that:

Acked-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that storing the memcg stats in every pool is inefficient, as these values are always the same for all zswap pools. They suggested introducing a new struct zs_memcg_params to store these values and passing it from zswap.c while passing NULL from zram.c.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner suggested that the patch should be modified to set pool->memcg_params and use it to gate in zsmalloc.c, indicating a need for additional code changes.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed liked the patch and appreciated the suggested improvement to zs_create_pool() prototype, which adds memcg awareness to compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat enum indices.
review_comments.raw_body:
I like this.

I also didn't like the suggested prototype for zs_create_pool(), and
didn't like that compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat did not have
memcg anywhere in the name. Was going to suggest adding a warning if
memcg_aware=false but the stat indices are non-zero, but your
suggestion is so much cleaner.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that zram does not support memcg accounting and modified the zs_create_pool call to reflect this, agreeing to a change in v2.

Author agreed to remove the 'memcg_aware' field and instead use bitwise OR of compressed_stat and uncompressed_stat, acknowledging that 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item

Author agrees to restructure the code to address Johannes Weiner's feedback, promising to make changes in version 3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Johannes,

Thank you for your review! I hope you are doing well : -)

---

Agreed. Originally I thought of removing memcg_aware and doing a bitwise
OR check of the two stats, but thought this was also a bit strange
(also because 0 is not a valid enum state for memcg_stat_item anyways)

---

These definitely look a lot cleaner. Will make these changes in v3!

Thanks again. I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce objcgs pointer in struct zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an array of obj_cgroup pointers in the zspage structure to track compressed objects' memory cgroup ownership, if the zs_pool is made memcg-aware at creation time. The error path for alloc_zspage has been simplified by moving it to a jump label. Additionally, the patch includes changes to handle zpdesc allocation and free_zpdesc calls in the error path.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested using vmalloc instead of kcalloc for allocating the objcgs array, allowing a fallback to vmalloc if necessary
review_comments.raw_body:
I remembered asking this, so my apologies if I missed/forgot your
response - but would vmalloc work here? i.e kvcalloc to fallback to
vmalloc etc.?

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the vmalloc call should be changed to use GFP_KERNEL instead of GFP_HIGHMEM, agreed to make this change in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Nhat : -)

Thank you for reviewing, and for your acks on the other parts!

You're right, I missed changing that on my end after v1. No reason
vmalloc shouldn't work here, let me make that change in v3.

Thanks, I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/11] mm/zsmalloc: Store obj_cgroup pointer in zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to store the obj_cgroup pointer in zspage, enabling the propagation of object group information from the zswap and zram layers down to zsmalloc. The patch modifies several kernel files, including drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c, include/linux/zsmalloc.h, and mm/zsmalloc.c, to accommodate this change.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue where writing to uninitialized memory could occur if pool->memcg_aware is not set. Suggested adding a conditional statement to avoid this write.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the suggested change and agreed to implement it in the next version of the patch series.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 11 Mar 2026 13:17:26 -0700 Yosry Ahmed <yosry@kernel.org> wrote:

> [..]
> > @@ -1216,6 +1216,11 @@ void zs_obj_write(struct zs_pool *pool, unsigned long handle,
> >         class = zspage_class(pool, zspage);
> >         off = offset_in_page(class->size * obj_idx);
> >
> > +       if (objcg) {
> > +               WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware);
> > +               zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;
> > +       }

Hello Yosry,

I hope you are doing well. Thank you for reviewing this series! : -)

> If pool->memcg_aware is not set the warning will fire, but the
> following line will write to uninitialized memory and probably crash.
> We should avoid the write if the warning fires.
> 
> Maybe:
> 
> if (objcg && !WARN_ON_ONCE(!pool->memcg_aware))
>        zspage->objcgs[obj_idx] = objcg;

Ack. 

> Not pretty, but the same pattern is followed in many places in the kernel.
> 
> > +
> >         if (!ZsHugePage(zspage))
> >                 off += ZS_HANDLE_SIZE;
> >

Definitely better than writing garbage and crashing : -)
I'll make this change in the next version, I think I should also sprinkle
these WARN_ON_ONCEs in a few other places as well. I'll be more
mindful of this for those cases as well.

Thank you again Yosry, I hope you have a great day!
Joshua

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch redirects the zswap layer to use the obj_cgroup pointer stored in the zspage, eliminating duplicate storage and resulting in a net zero memory footprint change.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the impact of this change on zswap's memory usage and suggested further analysis is needed to ensure it doesn't introduce any regressions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the zsmalloc and zswap modules to handle object charging and lifetime management correctly, aligning with obj_cgroup changes. It introduces a new charging mechanism based on size_class slots rather than compressed object sizes.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the new charging mechanism and suggested additional checks to ensure accurate memory accounting. Also pointed out that some functions are missing EXPORT_SYMBOL tags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/11] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve memory accounting for compressed objects in the zswap cache by tracking MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes, allowing for sub-PAGE_SIZE granularity charging.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Nhat Pham gave a positive review of the patch, stating it looks good to them (LGTM) and providing a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 11, 2026 at 12:52 PM Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Zswap compresses and uncompresses in PAGE_SIZE units, which simplifies
> the accounting for how much memory it has compressed. However, when a
> compressed object is stored at the boundary of two zspages, accounting
> at a PAGE_SIZE granularity makes it difficult to fractionally charge
> each backing zspage with the ratio of memory it backs for the
> compressed object.
>
> To make sub-PAGE_SIZE granularity charging possible for MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> track the value in bytes and adjust its accounting accordingly.
>
> No functional changes intended.
>
> Signed-off-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

LGTM.
Reviewed-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

> ---
>  include/linux/memcontrol.h | 2 +-
>  mm/memcontrol.c            | 5 +++--
>  mm/zsmalloc.c              | 4 ++--
>  mm/zswap.c                 | 8 +++++---
>  4 files changed, 11 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/include/linux/memcontrol.h b/include/linux/memcontrol.h
> index 701d9ab6fef1..ce2e598b5963 100644
> --- a/include/linux/memcontrol.h
> +++ b/include/linux/memcontrol.h
> @@ -38,7 +38,7 @@ enum memcg_stat_item {
>         MEMCG_VMALLOC,
>         MEMCG_KMEM,
>         MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> -       MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> +       MEMCG_ZSWAPPED_B,
>         MEMCG_NR_STAT,
>  };
>
> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> index 68139be66a4f..1cb02d2febe8 100644
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
> @@ -342,7 +342,7 @@ static const unsigned int memcg_stat_items[] = {
>         MEMCG_VMALLOC,
>         MEMCG_KMEM,
>         MEMCG_ZSWAP_B,
> -       MEMCG_ZSWAPPED,
> +       MEMCG_ZSWAPPED_B,
>  };
>
>  #define NR_MEMCG_NODE_STAT_ITEMS ARRAY_SIZE(memcg_node_stat_items)
> @@ -1364,7 +1364,7 @@ static const struct memory_stat memory_stats[] = {
>         { "shmem",                      NR_SHMEM                        },
>  #ifdef CONFIG_ZSWAP
>         { "zswap",                      MEMCG_ZSWAP_B                   },
> -       { "zswapped",                   MEMCG_ZSWAPPED                  },
> +       { "zswapped",                   MEMCG_ZSWAPPED_B                },
>  #endif
>         { "file_mapped",                NR_FILE_MAPPED                  },
>         { "file_dirty",                 NR_FILE_DIRTY                   },
> @@ -1412,6 +1412,7 @@ static int memcg_page_state_unit(int item)
>         switch (item) {
>         case MEMCG_PERCPU_B:
>         case MEMCG_ZSWAP_B:
> +       case MEMCG_ZSWAPPED_B:
>         case NR_SLAB_RECLAIMABLE_B:
>         case NR_SLAB_UNRECLAIMABLE_B:
>                 return 1;
> diff --git a/mm/zsmalloc.c b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> index 291194572a09..24665d7cd4a9 100644
> --- a/mm/zsmalloc.c
> +++ b/mm/zsmalloc.c
> @@ -1047,7 +1047,7 @@ static void zs_charge_objcg(struct zs_pool *pool, struct obj_cgroup *objcg,
>         rcu_read_lock();
>         memcg = obj_cgroup_memcg(objcg);
>         mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->compressed_stat, size);
> -       mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->uncompressed_stat, 1);
> +       mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->uncompressed_stat, PAGE_SIZE);
>         rcu_read_unlock();
>  }
>
> @@ -1066,7 +1066,7 @@ static void zs_uncharge_objcg(struct zs_pool *pool, struct obj_cgroup *objcg,
>         rcu_read_lock();
>         memcg = obj_cgroup_memcg(objcg);
>         mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->compressed_stat, -size);
> -       mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->uncompressed_stat, -1);
> +       mod_memcg_state(memcg, pool->uncompressed_stat, -(int)PAGE_SIZE);

nit: seems a bit awkward lol?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the tracking of zswap statistics to improve accuracy and granularity. It moves the ZSWAP_B and ZSWAPPED_B counters from memcg_stat_item to node_stat_item, allowing for more precise tracking at a finer granularity. However, this change introduces an inaccuracy in NR_ZSWAPPED_B due to the coarse PAGE_SIZE granularity of node stats.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the inaccuracy introduced by moving ZSWAP_B and ZSWAPPED_B counters. Suggested exploring alternative solutions to improve accuracy without introducing new inaccuracies.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses memory migration issues in the zsmalloc subsystem, specifically handling single object charge migration and transfer of lruvec and node statistics during migration. The approach taken is to simplify the migration process by uncharging entirely from the source nodes and charging entirely to the destination nodes, rather than enumerating all possible combinations of node migrations.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's handling of node migrations and suggested using a more straightforward approach to simplify the code. Also pointed out that the patch does not handle the case where the compressed object spans multiple pages.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged David's concerns and suggested a revised approach to handle node migrations. Also pointed out that the patch needs to be updated to handle the case where the compressed object spans multiple pages.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses charge migration in zsmalloc by handling zpdesc substitutions, which may change the node of compressed objects and their lruvec association. It takes special care to address partial compressed objects at the beginning and end of swapped-out zpdescs.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's handling of partial compressed objects and suggested adding additional checks to ensure correct behavior.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 6/6] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Discussion around the memory.high tier-aware patch, focusing on its interaction with the standard memory reclaim infrastructure and potential infinite loop issues.

review_comments.author: Bing Jiao
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about potential infinite loop issues when memory.high is breached and suggested introducing a fix orthogonal to the patch series.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Clarified the patch's behavior and interaction with the standard memory reclaim infrastructure, addressing Bing's concerns about infinite loop issues.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch handles objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc, but a reviewer pointed out that the obj_cgroup_get() reference is for the pointer, not the charge.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Raised concern about the clarity of code, specifically the use of obj_cgroup_get() for the pointer, not the charge. Suggested reordering the obj_cgroup_get() calls to make it clearer.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch proposes adding a sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth, but the discussion revolves around its feasibility and whether it should be posted as an RFC.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Suggested that userland could perform a simple calculation to determine the preferred weighting, but ultimately recommended posting the patch as an RFC due to concerns about its upstream viability.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/mempolicy: add sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch proposes adding a sysfs interface to override NUMA node bandwidth, but the discussion revolves around its feasibility and whether it should be posted as an RFC.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Suggested that userland could perform a simple calculation to determine the preferred weighting, but ultimately recommended posting the patch as an RFC due to concerns about its upstream viability.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: JP Kobryn suggests simplifying the policy triplet counters to a single aggregated counter per node, addressing concerns about signal and stats placement.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (Meta)
review_comments.summary: Proposed simplifying policy triplet counters to a single aggregated counter per node, addressing concerns about signal and stats placement.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 1/7] selftests/cgroup: skip test_zswap if zswap is globally disabled
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the logic for skipping a test when zswap is globally disabled by consolidating the skip and fail conditions into one place.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested consolidating the logic for checking zswap configuration into a single helper function, check_zswap_enabled(), which would read and parse the enabled value in one place.

review_comments.author: Li Wang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Li Wang suggested consolidating the logic for deciding whether to skip or fail the test based on zswap_enabled() in one place, and proposed opening up the code if no other callers exist.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, pass it as &value works well.

Patchset v3 is on the way :).

-- 
Regards,
Li Wang

review_comments.author: Li Wang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that ksft_exit_skip() is used inconsistently in zswap_enabled(), sometimes returning a value and other times using it to decide whether to skip or fail, and suggested consolidating the logic into one place.
review_comments.raw_body:
And use sizeof(&value) just get the pointer's size but not
the buffer size, so I would recommend: char value[2].

-- 
Regards,
Li Wang

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/5] selftests/cgroup: avoid OOM in test_swapin_nozswap
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch modifies the selftests/cgroup test_swapin_nozswap to avoid out-of-memory (OOM) issues by potentially changing constant values to multiples of the base page size, rather than fixed values.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Questioned the scope of the contended_release tracepoint, suggesting use of waitqueue_active() for consistency with other calls.
